paper_title: The Last Harness You'll Ever Build

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: AI agents are increasingly deployed on complex, domain-specific workflows -- navigating enterprise web applications that require dozens of clicks and form fills, orchestrating multi-step research pipelines that span search, extraction, and synthesis, automating code review across unfamiliar repositories, and handling customer escalations that demand nuanced domain knowledge. \textbf{Each new task domain requires painstaking, expert-driven harness engineering}: designing the prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and evaluation criteria that make a foundation model effective. We present a two-level framework that automates this process. At the first level, the \textbf{Harness Evolution Loop} optimizes a worker agent's harness $\mathcal{H}$ for a single task: a Worker Agent $W_{\mathcal{H}}$ executes the task, an Evaluator Agent $V$ adversarially diagnoses failures and scores performance, and an Evolution Agent $E$ modifies the harness based on the full history of prior attempts. At the second level, the \textbf{Meta-Evolution Loop} optimizes the evolution blueprint $Λ= (W_{\mathcal{H}}, \mathcal{H}^{(0)}, V, E)$ itself across diverse tasks, \textbf{learning a blueprint $Λ^{(\text{best})}$ that enables rapid harness convergence on any new task -- so that adapting an agent to a novel domain requires no human harness engineering at all.} We formalize the correspondence to meta-learning and present both algorithms. The framework \textbf{shifts manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering}, and takes one step further -- \textbf{automating the design of the automation itself}.

sections.title: Technical Report
sections.content: THE LAST HARNESS YOULL EVER BUILD Haebin Seong Sylph.AI Li Yin Sylph.AI Haoran Zhang Sylph.AI

sections.title: ABSTRACT
sections.content: AI agents are increasingly deployed on complex, domain-specific workflows navigating enterprise web applications that require dozens of clicks and form fills, orchestrating multi-step research pipelines that span search, extraction, and synthesis, automating code review across unfamiliar repositories, and handling customer escalations that demand nuanced domain knowledge. Each new task domain requires painstaking, expert-driven harness engineering: designing the prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and evaluation criteria that make foundation model effective. We present two-level framework that automates this process. At the first level, the Harness Evolution Loop optimizes worker agents harness for single task: Worker Agent WH executes the task, an Evaluator Agent adversarially diagnoses failures and scores performance, and an Evolution Agent modifies the harness based on the full history of prior attempts. At the second level, the Meta-Evolution Loop optimizes the evolution blueprint Λ = (WH, H(0), V, E) itself across diverse tasks, learning blueprint Λ(best) that enables rapid harness convergence on any new taskso that adapting an agent to novel domain requires no human harness engineering at all. We formalize the correspondence to meta-learning and present both algorithms. The framework shifts manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering, and takes one step furtherautomating the design of the automation itself.

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.content: Recent work on harness engineering has demonstrated that carefully designed scaffolding execution environments, feedback loops, evaluation criteria, and context managementcan dramatically amplify what agents achieve (Lopopolo, 2026; Rajasekaran, 2026). However, these harnesses are themselves products of highly intensive, specialized human engineering. Lopopolo (2026) describe building custom linters, repository-local observability stacks (logs, metrics, traces), Chrome DevTools integration, and structured documentation hierarchiesall hand-crafted to make the codebase legible to the agent. Rajasekaran (2026) report iterating through multiple rounds of evaluator prompt calibration with few-shot examples, designing four grading criteria for subjective design quality, and building three-agent planner-generator-evaluator architecture with sprint In both cases, the harness required deep domain expertise contracts negotiated between agents. to construct and significant iteration to tune. The harness improves the agent, but improving the harness still requires substantial human expertise applied to each specific task domain. While automated prompt optimization methods such as LLM-AutoDiff (Yin & Wang, 2025) can tune individual components, they do not address the full harnessthe tools, orchestration logic, infrastructure, and their interactions. We propose two-level framework that automates this improvement cycle. At the first level, the Harness Evolution Loop optimizes worker agents harness for single task through closedloop cycle of three agents: 1. Worker Agent WHthe agent under optimizationparameterized by its harness H, which executes task and produces an execution trace. 2. An Evaluator Agent that adversarially verifies task outcomes, diagnoses failure modes, and scores performance.

sections.title: Technical Report
sections.content: Figure 1: System architecture. The Meta-Evolution Loop (green, outer) optimizes the evolution blueprint Λ by running the Harness Evolution Loop (blue, inner) across diverse training tasks t1, t2, . . . , tn. Each inner loop instance optimizes worker harness for single task through iterative cycles of execution (Worker), evaluation (Evaluator), and code modification (Evolution Agent). The meta-evolution agent aggregates scores across all tasks and modifies the evolution blueprint, feeding the updated blueprint back for the next round. The output is the best-performing evolution blueprint Λ(best). 3. An Evolution Agent that analyzes the full evolution history and modifies the harness prompts, tools, orchestration logic, observations, and model configurationto address diagnosed failure patterns. Starting from an initial harness H(0)which may be generic, untuned agent scaffoldthe loop iterates for steps: at each step, the worker executes the task, the evaluator diagnoses and scores the result, and the evolution agent produces an improved harness based on the full history of prior attempts, ultimately returning the best-performing harness H(best). Together, these components form an evolution blueprint Λ = (WH, H(0), V, E) that fully specifies how the harness is evolved. At the second level, Meta-Evolution Loop optimizes Λ itself across diverse tasks, learning an evolution blueprint Λ(best) that enables rapid harness convergence on any new tasktransforming not just harness engineering, but the design of the harness engineering process itself, into an automated optimization problem.

sections.title: 2 THE HARNESS EVOLUTION LOOP
sections.content: We first formally define the notion of an agent harness, then describe the systems componentstask definition, task execution, evaluation, and evolutionand how they are orchestrated in the harness evolution loop. The four components are orchestrated by continuous automation loop, shown in Algorithm 1.

sections.title: Technical Report
sections.content: Algorithm 1 The Harness Evolution Loop Require: Task t, Worker Agent WH, initial worker harness H(0), Evaluator Agent , Evolution Agent E, iterations Rebuild WH(k1) from H(k1) Prepare target environment; reset to clean state trace WH(k1) .execute(t) {Worker runs task} (report, score) V.evaluate(trace, t) {Evaluator diagnoses and scores} if score > best score then 1: H(best) H(0); best score 2: history [ ] {Log of (H(k), report, score, verdict) per iteration} 3: for = 1, 2, . . . , do 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: 10: 11: end if 12: history history {(H(k1), report, score, verdict)} 13: 14: H(k) E.evolve(history, H(best)) {Evolve from best harness} 15: end for 16: return H(best), best score, history verdict IMPROVED; H(best) H(k1); best score score verdict REGRESSED else Algorithm 2 The Meta-Evolution Loop Require: Meta-train tasks Ttrain, Meta-Evolution Agent Emeta, initial evolution blueprint Λ(0), inner-loop budget , best scorei, historyi HARNESSEVOLUTIONLOOP(ti, Λ(j), K) {Alg. 1} end for task results [ ] for each task ti Ttrain do H(best) task results task results {(ti, best scorei, historyi)} 1: Λ(best) Λ(0); best meta score 2: meta history [ ] 3: for = 0, 1, 2, . . . do 4: 5: 6: 7: 8: 9: meta score Aggregate(task results) {Mean score across tasks} 10: 11: 12: 13: 14: 15: meta history meta history {(Λ(j), task results, meta score, verdict)} 16: 17: end for 18: return Λ(best), best meta score, meta history verdict IMPROVED; Λ(best) Λ(j); best meta score meta score Λ(j+1) Emeta.evolve(meta history, Λ(best)) {Evolve from best blueprint} if meta score > best meta score then verdict REGRESSED end if else 2.1 DEFINING THE AGENT HARNESS is not an agent. the formulation: raw model Agent = Model + Harness. harness is every piece of code, configuration, and execution logic that is not the model itselfit is the system that makes the models intelligence useful. harness can take many forms; popular categories of harness components include: Following Trivedy (2026), we adopt System prompts and task prompts: system-level instructions that define the agents identity and constraints, and task-level prompts that specify goals, success criteria, and incontext examples. Tools, skills, and their descriptions: the capabilities the agent can invoke to act on its environment (e.g., file editing, shell execution, UI interaction, web search, MCP servers).

sections.title: Technical Report
sections.content: Bundled infrastructure: the execution environment provided to the agent (filesystem, sandboxes, browsers, observability stacks). Orchestration logic: the control flow that structures the agents interaction loop (subagent spawning, handoffs, model routing, feedback loops, and continuation patterns such as the Ralph Loop). Hooks and middleware: deterministic execution guarantees injected around the model (compaction, continuation, lint checks, verification loops). Model configurations: the choice of underlying model, inference parameters (temperature, sampling strategy, token limits), and model routing rules that determine which model handles which subtask. Harnesses appear throughout the agent ecosystem. AdaL (SylphAI, 2026), Claude Code (Anthropic, 2025), and Codex (OpenAI, 2025) are harnesses for general-purpose software engineeringthey wrap LLMs with filesystem access, shell execution, web search, and multi-file editing. OpAgent (Guo et al., 2026) is harness for autonomous web navigation, combining Planner, Grounder, Reflector, and Summarizer into multi-agent pipeline that achieved state-of-the-art results on WebArena (Zhou et al., 2024). In every case, the harnessnot the modeldetermines what the agent can perceive, how it acts, and how its work is orchestrated and verified. 2.2 TASK DEFINITIONS task = (I, S) consists of: Instructions I: concrete goal for the worker agent. Success criteria = {s1, s2, . . . , sm}: checklist of verifiable conditions the evaluator uses to judge completion. 2.3 WORKER AGENT The Worker Agent WH is the agent under optimizationparameterized by its harness H. It exposes single interface WH.execute(t): given task t, the worker receives the instructions I, interacts with the target environment through tool interface, and produces an execution trace τ containing environment observations, action logs, and timing information for each step. The harness-based agents described in Section 2.1AdaL, Claude Code, Codex, and OpAgent can all be set as worker agents WH, each attempting to solve task using specific harness configuration. 2.4 EVALUATOR AGENT The Evaluator Agent is separate, adversarial It exposes the interface V.evaluate(τ, t) (report, score): given an execution trace τ and the original task = (I, S), it produces structured diagnostic report and numerical score. The evaluator performs four functions: reviewer. 1. State verification: Cross-references the workers observations in τ with ground-truth environment state to confirm the agent actually perceived what it claims, detecting hallucinated or misinterpreted states. 2. Criteria checking: Evaluates the workers final state against each success criterion si S, producing pass/fail verdict per criterion. 3. Performance auditing: Decomposes total execution time into LLM time (model inference latency) versus tool time (environment interaction latency), identifying whether bottlenecks are computational or behavioral. 4. Scoring: Computes two-tier metricfirst by pass/fail (whether the task was completed successfully), then by execution time as tiebreaker. This ranking determines whether code change represents net improvement or regression.

sections.title: 2.5 EVOLUTION AGENT
sections.content: The Evolution Agent is the evolutionary driver of the system. It exposes the interface E.evolve(history, H(best)) H: given the full evolution history and the best-performing harness, it produces modified harness H. It operates as senior engineer that: 1. Aggregates diagnostics: Reads the full evolution historyincluding what harness variants were tried, their evaluator reports, scores, and whether each change improved or regressed performance. This historical context prevents the evolution agent from repeating unsuccessful strategies and enables it to build on prior insights. 2. Identifies failure patterns: Classifies failures into recurring categories (e.g., incorrect tool usage, reasoning loops, misinterpreted environment state, excessive latency). 3. Modifies the harness: Based on the diagnosed failure patterns, the evolution agent edits the workers harness Hevery piece of code and configuration that constitutes the agent except the models parametersincluding tool implementations, system prompts, orchestration logic, observation structure, or model configuration to address root causes.

sections.title: 3 META-EVOLUTION: LEARNING TO EVOLVE HARNESSES
sections.content: The Harness Evolution Loop as described optimizes the worker harness for single fixed task. But the harness evolution loop itselfthe evaluator prompt, the evolution agents diagnostic strategy, the scoring function, the observation structure, and the orchestration logicis also harness, which we denote Λ. Formally: Λ = (WH, H(0), V, E) (1) where WH is the worker agent, H(0) is the initial worker harness, is the evaluator agent, and is the evolution agent. Together, these components define how the loop operates. In the current system, Λ is designed by human engineers and remains fixed throughout the evolution process. We now describe natural generalization: Meta-Evolution Agent that optimizes Λ itself, so that the inner harness evolution loop converges faster and more reliably to high-performing worker harnesses across diverse tasks. 3.1 THE HARNESS EVOLUTION LOOP AS HARNESS Observe that Λ has exactly the same structure as any other harness: it consists of prompts (the evaluator and evolution agent instructions), tools (the scoring function, version control operations, code editing capabilities), observations (what telemetry and traces are surfaced from the worker, evaluator, and evolution agent), and orchestration logic (how many iterations to run, when to commit or revert, how tasks are selected and ordered). Optimizing Λ is therefore harness optimization at higher level of abstraction. The components of Λ that the Meta-Evolution Agent can modify include: Evaluator agent promptwhat failure modes to look for, how to grade, what evidence to require. Evolution agent prompthow to diagnose failure patterns, what code changes to prioritize, how aggressively to modify the worker. Worker observation structurewhat telemetry, traces, and intermediate state to surface from the workers execution. Evaluator and evolution agent observationswhat information flows between agents at each step. Scoring function designthe metric structure (e.g., two-tier vs. multi-dimensional), thresholds, and tiebreakers. Loop hyperparametersnumber of iterations, parallelism, revert thresholds, and stopping criteria. 3.2 META-LEARNING FORMULATION This two-level optimization maps directly onto the meta-learning framework (Thrun & Pratt, 1998). Let Ttrain = {t1, t2, . . . , tn} be set of meta-train tasks, each representing different agent task

sections.title: Technical Report
sections.content: from potentially different domains. Let Ttest be held-out set of meta-test tasks used to evaluate generalization. The two loops operate as follows: Inner loop (the Harness Evolution): Given fixed harness evolution blueprint Λ and single task ti, run the harness evolution loop for iterations to produce an optimized worker harness H(K). Measure the convergence trajectory: how quickly and how well the worker improves on this task. Outer loop (the Meta-Evolution): Across multiple tasks ti Ttrain, evaluate how effectively the current Λ drives the inner loop. Modify Λ to improve the speed of adaptation the rate at which the inner loop converges to high performance on any single task. The objective of the outer loop is to find harness evolution blueprint Λ(best) that maximizes task performance across training tasks: Λ(best) = arg max (cid:2)best score(cid:0)HARNESSEVOLUTIONLOOP(ti, Λ, K)(cid:1)(cid:3) EtiTtrain (2) Λ where HARNESSEVOLUTIONLOOP(ti, Λ, K) runs Algorithm 1 for iterations, returning the bestperforming harness H(best), its score, and the full evolution history. The evolution blueprint Λ is judged solely by the final best score achieved on each tasknot by intermediate progress. This formulation is shown in Algorithm 2, and mirrors meta-learning, with the correspondence shown in Table 1. Table 1: Correspondence between meta-learning and meta-evolution Meta-Learning Meta-Evolution Harness being evolved: Parameters being adapted: θ Adaptation procedure (θ(0), optimizer, loss) Evolution blueprint Λ = (WH, H(0), V, E) Inner loop: gradient updates on task ti Outer loop: meta-gradient update Meta-train tasks Meta-test tasks Objective: fast adaptation to new tasks Inner loop: HARNESSEVOLUTIONLOOP(ti, Λ, K) Outer loop: Emeta.evolve(meta history, Λ(best)) Training tasks Ttrain Held-out tasks Ttest Objective: fast harness convergence on new tasks 3.3 EVALUATION PROTOCOL Generalization is evaluated on Ttest: given new, unseen task, how quickly does the inner harness evolution loopconfigured with the learned Λ(best)produce high-performing worker harness? The key metrics are: Convergence speed: number of inner-loop iterations to reach target performance threshold. Final performance: task pass rate after fixed number of iterations. Robustness: variance in convergence speed across different meta-test tasks. well-optimized Λ(best) should enable the harness evolution loop to rapidly adapt to any novel taskproducing effective worker harnesses with fewer iterations and less compute than manually designed harness evolution loop.

sections.title: 4 CONCLUSION
sections.content: We presented the Harness Evolution Loop, closed-loop architecture that automatically optimizes an AI agents harness Hthe prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and infrastructure that surround foundation modelthrough repeated task execution, adversarial evaluation, and code modification. The system formalizes the agent as WH, separates evaluation (V ) from evolution (E), and iteratively improves while tracking the full convergence history.

sections.title: Technical Report
sections.content: We then introduced the Meta-Evolution Loop, which optimizes the evolution blueprint Λ = (WH, H(0), V, E) itself across diverse tasks. By running the inner harness evolution loop on each training task ti Ttrain and measuring convergence, the meta-evolution agent Emeta learns blueprint Λ(best) that enables rapid adaptation to unseen tasks. This two-level formulation mirrors metathe inner loop adapts the harness to single task, while the outer loop optimizes the learning: adaptation procedure for generalization. Where harness engineering has traditionally required deep human expertise applied to each specific task domain, the Harness Evolution Loop automates this process entirelytransforming manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering. The Meta-Evolution Loop takes this one step further: it automates the design of the automation itself, learning how to evolve harnesses rather than evolving any single harness. We plan to follow up with empirical results on diverse workflows that have resisted easy automation even with state-of-the-art agents and harnessesfrom complex customized customer workflows to domain-specific enterprise processesdemonstrating that the framework can crack open task categories previously considered too brittle or too specialized for autonomous agents. Ultimately, we will release product built on the learned evolution blueprint Λ(best): system where any user can point general-purpose agent at new task domain and have it automatically evolve into specialized, high-performing agentno harness engineering expertise required.
